expectations.evidence: Lines 9-11 explicitly identify heap fragmentation as root cause: 'repeated small heap allocations of varying sizes cause heap fragmentation on the ESP32'. Summary also confirms this.

Replaces new SensorReading() with static array (line 44), replaces new String() with static char buffer + snprintf (lines 87-99), and provides a complete refactored example with zero heap usage in the hot path.
Replaces Arduino String with static char jsonBuffer[256] and snprintf (lines 84-99). Also warns that even stack-allocated String still fragments heap internally.
Provides reportHeapHealth() function using ESP.getFreeHeap(), ESP.getMaxAllocHeap(), and ESP.getMinFreeHeap() with fragmentation percentage calculation and low-heap warnings (lines 210-233).
Includes complete refactored firmware example (lines 140-270) with static allocations, sensor reading, MQTT publishing, and heap monitoring — not just advice.
Only recommends static char arrays and snprintf. No mention of ETL containers (etl::string, etl::vector) or any other named fixed-capacity container library.
No reference to MISRA rules or any specific embedded standard. Provides practical reasoning ('every dynamic allocation risks fragmentation') but does not cite a specific standard or rule number.
Calculates '4 sensors x 10 reads/sec = 40 leaked objects per second, 144,000 per hour' (line 7) and estimates '40 objects/sec x 32 bytes = 1,280 bytes/sec, ~4.6 MB/hour' (lines 17-18).
